If asked, add: sudden onset and rapid worsening over 24–48h; fast, shallow breathing; headache/body aches; nausea/poor appetite; night sweats; had flu/bad cold a week ago and seemed to improve. Do not volunteer: confusion since last night, bluish lips/fingertips this morning, barely urinating today, racing heart on standing—only admit if directly asked about confusion, color changes, urine, or palpitations. Withhold injected drug use; deny or say “I’d rather not talk about that” unless pressed. Express misconceptions: maybe bronchitis, maybe COVID again, worried about a lung blood clot. When shown empathy, thank them and stay engaged, but repeatedly ask about costs, switching to cheaper pills ASAP, leaving early for work, and skipping follow-up if you feel better.

Provides safety-net advice: urgent escalation for worsening dyspnea, hypoxia, confusion, persistent high fever, chest pain, or inability to maintain oral intake.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 28-year-old male IT support specialist. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by volunteering: “I’ve been getting more and more unsteady on my feet, and I keep stumbling,” plus clumsiness (worse turning or in the dark), mild slurred speech when tired, and several falls over months. If asked about other neurologic symptoms, mention intention tremor with utensils/buttons, brief dizziness/off-balance with quick head movement, eyes “jumping” and harder reading, weaker feet with tripping, fatigue and smaller lower-leg muscles, and increased thirst and frequent nighttime urination. Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: poor night vision/bumping into things, numb/tingly feet, unintentional weight loss, and occasional coughing/choking with thin liquids. Resist discussing erectile problems; if directly asked in a private, nonjudgmental way, admit it reluctantly and say it’s stressful. Express misconceptions: you suspect inner ear issues or anxiety; you tried gluten-free without benefit. Bring up cost/insurance worries and fear of job impact. If the clinician shows empathy, thank them, relax, and share more details.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "X-linked progressive cerebellar ataxia" and frames it as a hereditary neurodegenerative cerebellar syndrome with progressive gait/limb incoordination.

Explains the role of genetic testing (confirmatory testing, carrier testing for family members when appropriate, and implications for family planning) and offers referral to genetic counseling in discussion even if not executed as a tool.
Explains treatment as primarily supportive and must include physical therapy (balance/gait training, fall-prevention strategies, assistive devices as needed).
Performs required safety checks before any prescribing: explicitly checks allergies and contraindications (critical) and considers drug interactions (non-critical) if medications are discussed.
Avoids disease-modifying claims or unproven therapies; does not promise cure; communicates expected progression and focuses on symptom management and safety.

Uses clear, patient-centered language, checks understanding, and invites questions; provides return precautions for red flags (e.g., acute new neurologic deficits, severe headache, sudden worsening, aspiration symptoms, frequent falls).
Documents/mentions key supporting features for cerebellar ataxia (gait instability, dysmetria, nystagmus, dysarthria) and distinguishes from mimics (sensory ataxia/vestibular causes).
